The Basics

What Is an Indexed Universal Life Policy?

IUL in Plain English

An Indexed Universal Life policy is a permanent life insurance policy that does five things at once:

  • ✦ Provides a death benefit to protect your family
  • ✦ Builds cash value over time inside the policy
  • ✦ Links interest credits to a market index (like the S&P 500)
  • ✦ Protects against market losses with a 0% floor
  • ✦ Offers potential tax-advantaged access to your money
Permanent
Coverage for life — not just a term. It doesn't expire as long as the policy is properly funded.
0% Floor
When markets drop, your account is protected. You never lose principal or credited gains due to market declines.
Tax-Advantaged
Growth inside a life insurance contract may grow tax-deferred, with tax-favorable access strategies.
04 / 10
Policy illustrations are not guarantees of future performance.
Why People Choose IUL

Key Benefits of IUL

🛡
Death Benefit
Provides a generally income-tax-free payout to your family, ensuring financial security when it matters most.
📈
Index-Linked Growth
Cash value can grow based on market index performance, with no direct market exposure or ownership of stocks.
🔒
0% Loss Floor
When markets drop, your account is protected. You never lose principal or credited gains due to market declines.
💰
Flexible Premiums
Adjust your payments within policy guidelines to fit your budget and life changes over time.
🏥
Living Benefits
Many policies offer accelerated benefits for chronic, critical, or terminal illness — access your benefit while living.
🎓
Legacy Planning
Leave a meaningful inheritance or fund education for grandchildren in a tax-efficient manner.
05 / 10
Benefits vary by carrier and policy design. Consult your agent for policy-specific details. Living benefit riders may have additional costs.
Understanding the Engine

What Is a Market Index?

Think of It Like a Scoreboard

A market index — like the S&P 500 — is simply a scoreboard that tracks the performance of a group of stocks.

You don't OWN the stocks.
You don't LOSE money if they go down.

Instead, the interest your cash value earns is calculated based on how much the index went UP over a set period — up to a CAP.

If the index goes DOWN, you receive 0% interest — not a negative number. Your principal and locked-in gains are protected.

Annual Point-to-Point Example
Year Index Rule Applied You Earn
Year 1 +18% 10% Cap 10% Credited ✓
Year 2 −12% 0% Floor 0% Credited
Year 3 +7% 10% Cap 7% Credited ✓
Year 4 +22% 10% Cap 10% Credited ✓
Year 5 −4% 0% Floor 0% Credited

* Cap rates and participation rates vary by carrier and product. Simplified illustration only.

06 / 10
Hypothetical example for illustration purposes only. Not a guarantee of future index performance or interest credits.
Step by Step

How Annual Point-to-Point Crediting Works

1
Policy Anniversary
(Start Point)
Your policy anniversary date is recorded. The index value on that day becomes your starting point.
›
2
Index Tracks for 12 Months
Over the next year, the chosen index moves up or down. You don't own stocks — you're watching the scoreboard.
›
3
Next Anniversary
(End Point)
On your next policy anniversary, the index is measured again. The % change from start to end is calculated.
›
4
Floor & Cap
Applied
Positive? Capped at the policy cap (e.g. 10%). Zero or negative? The 0% floor applies. You never lose principal or your locked-in gains.
›
5
Interest
Credited
The calculated interest is credited to your cash value — permanently locked in. The cycle resets for next year.
✦   Key Takeaway: With Annual Point-to-Point, you participate in market GAINS up to a cap, and are completely shielded from market LOSSES.
07 / 10
Simplified illustration. Actual crediting depends on carrier, product design, participation rate, cap rate, and segment dates.
Why the Government Says Yes

The IRS Rules That Make It Work

These aren't loopholes — they're the law. Congress deliberately built tax advantages into life insurance to encourage Americans to protect their families and reduce dependence on government programs.

📜
IRC §101(a) — Tax-Free Death Benefit
When a life insurance policy pays out, your beneficiary receives it income-tax-free. A $500,000 401(k) could cost heirs 20–37% in taxes. A $500,000 death benefit? Every dollar.
📋
IRC §7702 — What Qualifies as Life Insurance
This is the IRS rulebook that defines what qualifies as life insurance. Stay within these guidelines and all tax benefits apply. Your agent and carrier handle compliance — you don't have to manage it.
📊
IRC §72(e) — Tax-Deferred Growth
Cash value inside a life insurance contract is not taxed each year. No annual 1099. It compounds without a tax drag — and policy loans are generally not considered taxable income.
⚖️
What You Can & Cannot Do
✔ Can: Grow tax-deferred · Access via policy loans · Pass death benefit tax-free · Supplement retirement income

✘ Cannot: Over-fund beyond IRS limits (creates a MEC) · Surrender with gains without tax consequence · Use as a short-term savings account
08 / 11
This is not tax advice. IRC §7702, §72(e), and §101(a) govern life insurance tax treatment. MEC rules apply to over-funded policies. Consult a licensed CPA or tax attorney for your situation.
Is It Right for You?

Why Should I Have an IUL?

IUL May Be Right for You If…
  • You want permanent life insurance coverage
  • You want to grow savings without risking market losses
  • You're looking for a supplemental retirement income stream
  • You want tax-advantaged accumulation beyond 401(k) limits
  • You're a business owner needing key-person or buy-sell coverage
  • You want to leave a tax-efficient legacy for your family
IUL vs. Other Safe Money Options
Product Pros Limitations
IUL ✦ 0% floor · Tax-adv. · Death benefit · Flexible premium · Avoids probate Requires proper structure · Long-term play
Savings Acct FDIC insured to limit · Liquid Low interest · No tax deferral · No death benefit
CD Fixed return · FDIC insured to limit No tax deferral · Penalty for early withdrawal · No death benefit
Market 401(k) High growth potential · Tax-deferred Market risk · Taxed at withdrawal · Penalty before age 59½
Term Life Low cost · Death benefit · Avoids probate Expires · No cash value
MYGA Guaranteed rate · Tax-deferred · Death benefit · Avoids probate Fixed rate only · Surrender charges may apply
FIA 0% floor · Index-linked · Tax-deferred · Death benefit · Avoids probate No living benefit · Surrender charges may apply
09 / 11
Comparisons are general and educational. Individual results vary. IUL is not a savings account or investment. Review all policy costs, risks, and benefits before purchasing.
Getting Money In

How Do You Fund an IUL Policy?

All IUL funding uses after-tax (non-qualified) dollars — which is actually an advantage. Because you've already paid tax going in, qualified access through policy loans is generally not taxed again.

📅
Monthly Premiums
The most common approach. Pay a set amount each month — just like any bill. Flexible within policy guidelines so if your budget changes, you have options.
📆
Annual Lump Sum
Write one check per year. A natural fit for business owners or anyone with variable income. Same after-tax dollars, same flexibility.
🏦
Premium Deposit Account (PDA)
Deposit a lump sum upfront. The carrier pays interest — locked in at the rate when received, typically around 4% depending on your state. Your annual premium is drawn from the PDA automatically. Many policies include Dollar Cost Averaging (DCA) — crediting your policy in monthly segments to smooth out index timing.
💡 PDA Example
$50,000
Deposited into PDA
~$10,800
Interest earned at 4% over 5 years
Net Savings
Your net premium cost reduced by interest earned
The carrier's money works for you — you earn interest while your premiums are paid. PDA interest rates vary by state and are locked in at time of deposit.
11 / 11
Funding options and PDA interest rates vary by carrier and state. Dollar Cost Averaging availability varies by product. This is not financial advice — consult your agent for policy-specific details.
